verses.text: After three years of peace, Jehoshaphat of Judah visits Ahab, who talks him into joining a war to retake Ramoth-gilead. The good king is drawn into an ungodly alliance. Friendship with the wicked, however pleasant, can pull a believer into ventures God never blessed.

After three years of peace, Jehoshaphat of Judah visits Ahab, who talks him into joining a war to retake Ramoth-gilead. The good king is drawn into an ungodly alliance. Friendship with the wicked, however pleasant, can pull a believer into ventures God never blessed.

Jehoshaphat asks for a prophet of the LORD, and Ahab admits he hates Micaiah, who never prophesies good of him. A king who only wants flattering oracles betrays a heart at war with truth. To resent the messenger who tells us hard things is to resent God Himself.

Jehoshaphat asks for a prophet of the LORD, and Ahab admits he hates Micaiah, who never prophesies good of him. A king who only wants flattering oracles betrays a heart at war with truth. To resent the messenger who tells us hard things is to resent God Himself.

Four hundred prophets promise victory, and Zedekiah waves iron horns to dramatize it. A loud, unanimous chorus is no proof of truth. Crowds of voices, even religious ones, can all be wrong together when they say only what the powerful want to hear.

Four hundred prophets promise victory, and Zedekiah waves iron horns to dramatize it. A loud, unanimous chorus is no proof of truth. Crowds of voices, even religious ones, can all be wrong together when they say only what the powerful want to hear.

The messenger urges Micaiah to agree with the rest, but he answers, 'what the LORD saith unto me, that will I speak.' One man stands against four hundred. The faithful servant is bound not to the majority but to the word God gives him, whatever the cost.

The messenger urges Micaiah to agree with the rest, but he answers, 'what the LORD saith unto me, that will I speak.' One man stands against four hundred. The faithful servant is bound not to the majority but to the word God gives him, whatever the cost.

Micaiah first echoes the others mockingly, then, pressed, tells the truth: Israel scattered like sheep with no shepherd. Ahab sulks. The prophet will not soothe a king bent on his own way; he loves him better with a hard truth than a sweet lie.

Micaiah unveils a vision: a lying spirit, permitted by God, fills the false prophets' mouths to lure Ahab to his doom. God can give the self-deceived over to the deceptions they prefer. Those who will not love the truth are left to the lie they chose.

Zedekiah strikes Micaiah, and Ahab jails him on bread and water, but the prophet warns, 'if thou return at all in peace, the LORD hath not spoken by me.' Persecuting the truth-teller cannot make his word untrue. The test will come at the battle's end.

Ahab disguises himself but sends Jehoshaphat into battle in royal robes; the Syrians nearly kill Jehoshaphat by mistake, and he cries out and is spared. Schemes to dodge God's word only entangle the innocent. No disguise will hide a man from the judgment God has decreed.

A soldier draws his bow 'at a venture' and the arrow finds the joint of Ahab's armour; he props himself up, bleeding, and dies at evening. The random shot was aimed by God. No armour, no disguise can turn aside the arrow the LORD has appointed.

Ahab is buried, and the dogs lick his blood at the pool of Samaria, just as Elijah foretold over Naboth's vineyard. The word against him is fulfilled to the letter. God's sentence, however long delayed, arrives precisely as He spoke it.

The account turns to Jehoshaphat, who walks in his father Asa's good ways, doing right in God's sight. After the dark tale of Ahab, here is a king who pleased the LORD. Even in evil times, God keeps a faithful witness on a throne.

Yet the high places are not removed, and Jehoshaphat makes peace with Israel — a mixed record of real godliness and unwise compromise. Even good men leave some ground unclaimed for God. Faithfulness in much does not excuse the corners we fail to surrender.

Jehoshaphat builds ships for gold, but they are wrecked at Ezion-geber and never sail. The venture, perhaps born of the same worldly alliances, comes to nothing. God sometimes breaks our schemes to spare us, refusing to prosper what would draw us from Him.

Ahaziah, Ahab's son, reigns in Israel and does evil, serving Baal like his parents. The wicked line continues unbroken. A house that will not repent simply hands its idols down, each generation inheriting the sins of the last.
